finding.contract_claim: getNodeLabel accesses node.children (line 18) and node.size/node.extension (line 21) on a value typed FsNode = FileNode | DirectoryNode, and each property exists on only one union member — accessing the wrong variant would yield undefined at runtime.
finding.observed_behavior: Each access is guarded by a discriminant check on node.type (line 17): the `directory` branch reads .children and the post-guard fall-through is narrowed to FileNode before reading .size/.extension.
finding.triage_reasoning:
The code at lines 16–22 of metrics.ts implements a textbook discriminated-union dispatch:

1. `FsNode` is `FileNode | DirectoryNode`, discriminated on the literal `type` field.
2. Line 17: `if (node.type === "directory")` — before *any* access to `node.children`, the discriminant is checked. Inside the `if`-body TypeScript (and runtime) knows `node` is `DirectoryNode`, so `.children` is always present.
3. Lines 20–21: after the `if`-block the discriminant check has already ruled out `DirectoryNode`, so TypeScript narrows `node` to `FileNode`. The accesses to `.size` and `.extension` are therefore safe; they can never be `undefined`.

The claim asserts a latent bug on the grounds that each property exists on only one union member, which is true in isolation but irrelevant here — the guard prevents access to the wrong member. Reality predicate fails: the gap does not exist. Dismissed as a false positive.
